Figure 18: Oracle prompt template used in the work.
Figure 17: Unified prompt template used in the work.

Table 13: Our best experimental results (avgstd). The mean accuracy scores with their standard deviation are computed by averaging the results of three independent runs with different random seeds. Results of baselines are also provided including random choice as well as the majority of class instances over each sub categorization tasks. The Bold and underlined texts denote the best and second-best performance in the specific category, respectively.

Figure 6: The ability of semantic relation categorization of LC with different numbers of in-context exemplars and semantic category scale. The number n of classes is chosen from N := {1, 2, 4, 8, 16}. Each model is prompted with the k-shot settings, where k ∈ {0, 3, 5}, respectively. Accuracy scores are calculated by the mean values based on 30 examples sampled per class from the test split of Espinosa-Anke et al. (2021), partial categories (n ≤ 8) are run with three-class combinations in random selection, finally result in the mean value as the average.

Table 6: Classification performance comparison. Cond.: accuracy given correct extraction; Overall: end-to-end accuracy. Green indicates the highest performance across all models (zero-shot and few-shot) within each task category, while red indicates the lowest.

Table 1: A summary of the data statistics in SEMANTICQA. P refers to the prompt template, S denotes the sentence context, T represents the semantic taxonomy narrative, IE denotes idiomatic expressions, LC denotes lexical collocations, and NC denotes noun compounds.
Figure 13: A data example of noun compound compositionality (NCC).
Table 14: Correspondence between the proposed semantic phrase subtypes and related categories in established MWE literature with representative references.
Table 7: We report the accuracy of selected frontier LMs on the VMWE extraction task under different ICL setups, both with and without ORACLE SCHEMA.

Table 9: All lexical functions with their semantic gloss in this paper. The column “semantic gloss” provides the definition for each LF, and we use a sentence to describe the complete meaning of LF in column “Complete Description”. K denotes the keyword/base word of a LF, and V denotes the value/collocate word of a LF.
